title: A mean-field description of strong-to-weak symmetry breaking in the monitored three-dimensional Bose-Hubbard model
authors: Yicheng Tang, Pradip Kattel, J. H. Pixley
abstract: Strong-to-weak spontaneous symmetry breaking has emerged as a novel form of ordering in monitored and open quantum systems, yet its characterization has so far primarily relied on nonlocal diagnostics. Here, we develop a Gutzwiller mean-field framework for monitored bosonic lattice systems, enabling the direct simulation of stochastic measurement dynamics in three spatial dimensions. Applying this approach to the monitored Bose-Hubbard model with local density measurements and Lindbladian dissipation, we identify strong-to-weak symmetry breaking through a trajectory-averaged local order parameter. We find that this local order parameter becomes critical near the same measurement strength as the charge-sharpening transition and exhibits Lorentz invariance with a correlation-length exponent, $ν\simeq 1.2$, comparable to that of the charge-sharpening transition, suggesting that the two phenomena may originate from a common underlying critical point. Our work establishes a local characterization of strong-to-weak symmetry breaking, reveals its connection to charge sharpening, and provides concrete predictions for future experiments on the monitored Bose-Hubbard model.
summary: This paper develops a mean-field theory to study symmetry breaking in monitored Bose-Hubbard systems. By analyzing local order parameters, the authors establish a local diagnostic for strong-to-weak symmetry breaking. They find that this transition is critically linked to the charge-sharpening transition, suggesting a unified critical physics in open quantum systems.
main_problem: The paper aims to characterize strong-to-weak spontaneous symmetry breaking (SWSSB) in open quantum systems and establish its connection to the charge-sharpening transition.
main_result: The local order parameter characterizing SWSSB is found to be critical near the same measurement strength as the charge-sharpening transition, suggesting they share a common underlying critical point.
method: The authors develop and apply a Gutzwiller mean-field framework to simulate the stochastic measurement dynamics of the Bose-Hubbard model.
model_or_system: The system is the monitored three-dimensional Bose-Hubbard model, whose dynamics are governed by a combination of the Bose-Hubbard Hamiltonian, Lindbladian dissipation, and local density measurements.
key_observables: Local order parameter, charge variance ($\delta n^2$), and the Rényi-2 correlator ($F$), which serve as diagnostics for SWSSB and charge sharpening, respectively.
important_parameters: Measurement strength ($\gamma$), dissipation rate ($D$), and the critical measurement rate ($\gamma_c$).
main_assumptions: The primary approximation is the use of the Gutzwiller Mean-Field Theory (GMFT), which assumes the state remains unentangled, simplifying the many-body problem to local single-site dynamics.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the Bose-Hubbard model dynamics across different measurement rates ($\gamma$), showing the evolution of the superfluid order parameter ($\Psi_i$) from coherent to number-eigenstate dominated regimes.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the model and the challenge of characterizing SWSSB. It develops the GMFT approach, applies it to analyze the local order parameter and charge variance, and concludes by establishing the critical connection between the two transitions.
why_interesting: This work provides a concrete, local diagnostic tool (the local order parameter) to study a complex non-equilibrium phenomenon (SWSSB) in a system relevant to quantum gas experiments, linking it to established critical phenomena.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Illustration of the Bose-Hubbard model with hopping J (blue arrows) and onsite interaction U (red arrows), subject to measurement (orange dashed lines) at rate γ and dissipation (green wavy lines) at rate D. The system is simulated using GMFT, where the local mean-field Hamiltonian at site i (unshaded) is obtained by tracing out all other sites (shaded) and depends on the mean field Φi defined in Eq. (5). The lower panel il- lustrates the interplay of measurement, Hamiltonian dy- namics, and dissipation. Measurement projects the state onto a number eigenstate |n⟩(single-color circle), de- stroying quantum coherence. Subsequent Hamiltonian evolution regenerates coherence...

FIG. 2: Panels (a)–(c) show the configuration of the superfluid order parameter Ψi = |Ψi|eiθi with arrows for a single shot in the steady state t = 50 with model parameter D = 0, U = J = 1, α = 0.2 and system size L = 30 for various measurement rate γ. The norm |Ψ| sets the length of a single arrow, and the arrow direction is determined by the phase in the direction (sin θ, cos θ, 0). (a) γ = 0 has broken symmetry with coherent phases θi = θj and Ψi = Ψj with a delta-function distribution in both variables over sites. (b) γ = 0.1 in the weak symmetry (charge fuzzy) phase, where |Ψi| ̸= 0 but with phases for each site that are randomly distributed. (c) γ = 0.9 at criticality. To visualize...

FIG. 3: Configuration of the local superfluid order pa- rameter Ψi for a single quantum trajectory at t = 50, with dissipation rate D = 0.1, U = J = 1, α = 0.2, and L = 30. Panels (a)–(c) show the evolution from the weak-symmetry to the strong-symmetry regime as γ increases. Unlike the nondissipative case, the system at γ = 0 evolves toward the infinite-temperature mixed state ρi ∝I, which is a weak-symmetry state. Panel (d) shows the decay of |¯Ψ(t)| ≃αe−(D/2+γ)t, indicated by the dashed line.

FIG. 4: Upper four panels (a-d) show the time evolution for two order parameters, with varying measurement rate γ. (a) shows F(t) with βF /νz = 2.252 ± 0.005 and (b) shows δn2(t) with βn/νz = 1.979 ± 0.004 at L = 24, α = 0.2 and D = 0. (c) shows F(t) with βF /νz = 2.253 ± 0.006 and (d) shows δn2(t) with βn/νz = 2.179 ± 0.008, at L = 14, α = 0.4 and D = 0.1. The dashed line is the fitting of the scaling behavior at criticality O(t) = A tβ/νz . The lower four panels (e-h) show the scaling behavior at time t = L, with the insets showing the data collapse using the scaling ansatz defined in Eq.9. (e) gives critical values γc = 0.894 ± 0.02, ν = 1.30 ± 0.06, (f) gives γc = 0.873 ± 0.01, ν = 1.18...

FIG. 5: The left and middle panels show the time evolution of the R´enyi-2 correlator F(t) for different local Hilbert- space truncations nmax, fillings α, and measurement rates γ. The right panels show the corresponding maximum occupation number max[n(t)]. All panels use the common color scheme indicated by the legend.
